diff (165 lines — TRUNCATED)
--- a//workspace/problems/07_w4a16_gemm/solution.py
+++ b//workspace/problems/07_w4a16_gemm/solution.py
@@ -44,16 +44,65 @@
 
 // One warp per output. Each lane reads 16 bytes (uint4) per K-iteration,
 // covering 32 K-elements; the warp covers 512 bytes = 1024 K = 8 groups/iter.
-// DEPTH-deep prefetch ring keeps DEPTH weight loads in flight to hide DRAM
-// latency (more MLP helps the low-occupancy small-N case).
-template<int DEPTH>
-__global__ void w4a16_gemv_kernel(
-    const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ x,
-    const uint8_t* __restrict__ wqt,
-    const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ sc,
-    const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ zz,
-    __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ y,
-    int N, int K, int KH) {
+// d1 = 1-deep prefetch (high-occupancy large-N), d2 = 2-deep (low-occupancy small-N).
+
+#define GEMV_BODY_PREFETCH1 \
+    int niters = KH >> 9; \
+    uint4 wn = *reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(wrow + 16 * lane); \
+    _Pragma("unroll 4") \
+    for (int it = 0; it < niters; it++) { \
+        uint4 wv = wn; \
+        int b = it * 512; \
+        if (it + 1 < niters) wn = *reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(wrow + b + 512 + 16 * lane); \
+        int g = b / 64 + (lane >> 2); \
+        float s = __bfloat162float(sc[(size_t)g * N + n]); \
+        float zf = __bfloat162float(zz[(size_t)g * N + n]); \
+        int k0 = 2 * b + 32 * lane; \
+        __nv_bfloat16 xb[32]; \
+        *reinterpret_cast<uint4*>(xb)      = *reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(x + k0); \
+        *reinterpret_cast<uint4*>(xb + 8)  = *reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(x + k0 + 8); \
+        *reinterpret_cast<uint4*>(xb + 16) = *reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(x + k0 + 16); \
+        *reinterpret_cast<uint4*>(xb + 24) = *reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(x + k0 + 24); \
+        const uint32_t* wp = reinterpret_cast<const uint32_t*>(&wv); \
+        _Pragma("unroll") \
+        for (int q = 0; q < 4; q++) { uint32_t p = wp[q]; int base = 8 * q; \
+            _Pragma("unroll") \
+            for (int j = 0; j < 4; j++) { unsigned int bv = (p >> (8 * j)) & 0xFFu; \
+                float xe = __bfloat162float(xb[base + 2 * j]); float xo = __bfloat162float(xb[base + 2 * j + 1]); \
+                float wl = ((float)(bv & 0xFu) - zf) * s; float wh = ((float)((bv >> 4) & 0xFu) - zf) * s; \
+                acc += xe * wl + xo * wh; } } }
+
+#define GEMV_BODY_PREFETCH2 \
+    int niters = KH >> 9; \
+    uint4 w0 = *reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(wrow + 0 + 16 * lane); \
+    uint4 w1 = (niters > 1) ? *reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(wrow + 512 + 16 * lane) : w0; \
+    _Pragma("unroll 4") \
+    for (int it = 0; it < niters; it++) { \
+        uint4 wv = w0; w0 = w1; \
+        if (it + 2 < niters) w1 = *reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(wrow + (it + 2) * 512 + 16 * lane); \
+        int b = it * 512; \
+        int g = b / 64 + (lane >> 2); \
+        float s = __bfloat162float(sc[(size_t)g * N + n]); \
+        float zf = __bfloat162float(zz[(size_t)g * N + n]); \
+        int k0 = 2 * b + 32 * lane; \
+        __nv_bfloat16 xb[32]; \
+        *reinterpret_cast<uint4*>(xb)      = *reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(x + k0); \
+        *reinterpret_cast<uint4*>(xb + 8)  = *reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(x + k0 + 8); \
+        *reinterpret_cast<uint4*>(xb + 16) = *reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(x + k0 + 16); \
+        *reinterpret_cast<uint4*>(xb + 24) = *reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(x + k0 + 24); \
+        const uint32_t* wp = reinterpret_cast<const uint32_t*>(&wv); \
+        _Pragma("unroll") \
+        for (int q = 0; q < 4; q++) { uint32_t p = wp[q]; int base = 8 * q; \
+            _Pragma("unroll") \
+            for (int j = 0; j < 4; j++) { unsigned int bv = (p >> (8 * j)) & 0xFFu; \
+                float xe = __bfloat162float(xb[base + 2 * j]); float xo = __bfloat162float(xb[base + 2 * j + 1]); \
+                float wl = ((float)(bv & 0xFu) - zf) * s; float wh = ((float)((bv >> 4) & 0xFu) - zf) * s; \
+                acc += xe * wl + xo * wh; } } }
+
+__global__ void w4a16_gemv_kernel_d1(
+    const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ x, const uint8_t* __restrict__ wqt,
+    const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ sc, const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ zz,
+    __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ y, int N, int K, int KH) {
     int wpb = blockDim.x >> 5;
     int warp = threadIdx.x >> 5;
     int lane = threadIdx.x & 31;
@@ -61,51 +110,28 @@
     if (n >= N) return;
     const uint8_t* wrow = wqt + (size_t)n * KH;
     float acc = 0.f;
-    int niters = KH >> 9;   // 512 bytes per warp-iter
-    uint4 wb[DEPTH];
+    GEMV_BODY_PREFETCH1
     #pragma unroll
-    for (int d = 0; d < DEPTH; d++)
-        if (d < niters) wb[d] = *reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(wrow + d * 512 + 16 * lane);
-    for (int it = 0; it < niters; it++) {
-        uint4 wv = wb[0];
-        #pragma unroll
-        for (int d = 0; d < DEPTH - 1; d++) wb[d] = wb[d + 1];
-        if (it + DEPTH < niters)
-            wb[DEPTH - 1] = *reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(wrow + (it + DEPTH) * 512 + 16 * lane);

-        int k0 = 2 * b + 32 * lane;

-        *reinterpret_cast<uint4*>(xb + 16) = *reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(x + k0 + 16);
-        *reinterpret_cast<uint4*>(xb + 24) = *reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(x + k0 + 24);
-        const uint32_t* wp = reinterpret_cast<const uint32_t*>(&wv);
-        #pragma unroll
-        for (int q = 0; q < 4; q++) {
-            uint32_t p = wp[q];
-            int base = 8 * q;
-            #pragma unroll

cat > ncu_gemm.py <<'EOF'
import torch, solution, reference
device='cuda:0'
M,N,K=32,12288,4096
reference.M,reference.N,reference.K=M,N,K
rm=reference.Model(M,N,K).to(device).eval()
sm=solution.Model(M,N,K).to(device).eval()
sm.load_state_dict(rm.state_dict(),strict=True)
x=torch.randn(M,K,dtype=torch.bfloat16,device=device)
for _ in range(5): sm(x)
torch.cuda.synchronize()
sm(x); torch.cuda.synchronize()
EOF

command (4030 chars — TRUNCATED)
cat > test_d3.py <<'EOF'
import torch
from torch.utils.cpp_extension import load_inline
src = r'''
#include <cuda_bf16.h>
#include <cstdint>
#include <torch/extension.h>
__global__ void gemv_d3(const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ x, const uint8_t* __restrict__ wqt,
    const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ sc, const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ zz,
    __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ y, int N, int K, int KH) {
    int wpb=blockDim.x>>5, warp=threadIdx.x>>5, lane=threadIdx.x&31;
    int n=blockIdx.x*wpb+warp; if(n>=N) return;
    const uint8_t* wrow=wqt+(size_t)n*KH; float acc=0.f; int niters=KH>>9;
    uint4 w0=*reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(wrow+0+16*lane);
    uint4 w1=(niters>1)?*reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(wrow+512+16*lane):w0;
    uint4 w2=(niters>2)?*reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(wrow+1024+16*lane):w1;
    _Pragma("unroll 4")
    for(int it=0;it<niters;it++){
        uint4 wv=w0; w0=w1; w1=w2;
        if(it+3<niters) w2=*reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(wrow+(it+3)*512+16*lane);
        int b=it*512; int g=b/64+(lane>>2);
        float s=__bfloat162float(sc[(size_t)g*N+n]); float zf=__bfloat162float(zz[(size_t)g*N+n]);
        int k0=2*b+32*lane; __nv_bfloat16 xb[32];
        *reinterpret_cast<uint4*>(xb)=*reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(x+k0);
        *reinterpret_cast<uint4*>(xb+8)=*reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(x+k0+8);
        *reinterpret_cast<uint4*>(xb+16)=*reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(x+k0+16);
        *reinterpret_cast<uint4*>(xb+24)=*reinterpret_cast<const uint4*>(x+k0+24);
        const uint32_t* wp=reinterpret_cast<const uint32_t*>(&wv);
        _Pragma("unroll")
        for(int q=0;q<4;q++){uint32_t p=wp[q];int base=8*q;
        _Pragma("unroll")
        for(int j=0;j<4;j++){unsigned int bv=(p>>(8*j))&0xFFu;
            float xe=__bfloat162float(xb[base+2*j]);float xo=__bfloat162float(xb[base+2*j+1]);
            float wl=((float)(bv&0xFu)-zf)*s;float wh=((float)((bv>>4)&0xFu)-zf)*s;
            acc+=xe*wl+xo*wh;}}}
    _Pragma("unroll")
    for(int off=16;off>0;off>>=1) acc+=__shfl_xor_sync(0xffffffff,acc,off);
    if(lane==0) y[n]=__float2bfloat16(acc);
}
void launch(torch::Tensor x, torch::Tensor wqt, torch::Tensor sc, torch::Tensor zz, torch::Tensor out, int wpb) {
    int N=out.size(1);int K=x.size(1);int KH=K/2;int block=wpb*32;int grid_n=(N+wpb-1)/wpb;
    gemv_d3<<<grid_n,block>>>(reinterpret_cast<const __nv_bfloat16*>(x.data_ptr()),
        reinterpret_cast<const uint8_t*>(wqt.data_ptr()),
        reinterpret_cast<const __nv_bfloat16*>(sc.data_ptr()),
        reinterpret_cast<const __nv_bfloat16*>(zz.data_ptr()),
        reinterpret_cast<__nv_bfloat16*>(out.data_ptr()),N,K,KH);
}
'''
mod=load_inline(name="w4d3",cpp_sources="void launch(torch::Tensor x, torch::Tensor wqt, torch::Tensor sc, torch::Tensor zz, torch::Tensor out, int wpb);",cuda_sources=src,functions=["launch"],verbose=False)
import reference
device='cuda:0'; flush=torch.empty(128*1024*1024//4,dtype=torch.float32,device=device)
def lf(): flush.zero_()
for M,N,K in [(1,4096,4096),(1,12288,4096)]:
    reference.M,reference.N,reference.K=M,N,K
    rm=reference.Model(M,N,K).to(device).eval(); wqt=rm.w_q.t().contiguous(); sc=rm.scales; zr=rm.zeros
    x=torch.randn(M,K,dtype=torch.bfloat16,device=device); out=torch.empty(M,N,dtype=torch.bfloat16,device=device)
    mod.launch(x,wqt,sc,zr,out,4); ref=rm(x); diff=(out.float()-ref.float()).abs().max().item()
    for wpb in [4,8]:
        for _ in range(20): mod.launch(x,wqt,sc,zr,out,wpb)
        torch.cuda.synchronize(); s=torch.cuda.Event(enable_timing=True); e=torch.cuda.Event(enable_timing=True); L=[]
        for _ in range(50): lf();torch.cuda.synchronize();s.record();mod.launch(x,wqt,sc,zr,out,wpb);e.record();torch.cuda.synchronize();L.append(s.elapsed_time(e))
        L.sort();ms=L[len(L)//2];by=M*K*2+(K//2)*N+(K//128)*N*2*2+M*N*2
        print(f"M={M} N={N} wpb={wpb}: {ms*1000:.1f}us {by/ms/1e6:.0f}GB/s frac={by/ms/1e6/1800:.3f} diff={diff:.1e}")
EOF

Let me assess the effort and risk. A wmma-based GEMM for small M: - wmma fragment: 16x16x16 bf16. - For BM=32, BN=128: tile is 2×8 wmma tiles (m16×n16). - Each warp could own one (16,16) output fragment, or multiple. - K-loop: load x[BM, BK] (shared), w_packed dequant to w[BK, BN] (shared/registers), wmma::mma. The dequant feeding wmma is the hard part — wmma load_matrix fragments expect a specific memory layout (row-major or col-major in shared mem). I'd dequant into a shared mem buffer in the right layout. This is ~150-200 lines of CUDA. Risk of bugs. But potentially big payoff. Alternatively — let me reconsider using CUTLASS. Let me try: reduce the live accumulator by processing the K-loop with a smaller effective tile. Or — the issue might be that Triton keeps BOTH x_lo, x_hi, w_lo, w_hi tiles live plus the acc. Let me try doing the two dots (lo, hi) more sequentially to reduce live registers. Actually, my GEMM does: ``` x = load (BM, BK) x_lo, x_hi = split # (BM, BK_HALF) w_packed = load (BK_HALF, BN) w_lo, w_hi = unpack # (BK_HALF, BN) each s, z = load w_lo = (w_lo - z)*s; w_hi = (w_hi - z)*s acc = dot(x_lo, w_lo, acc) acc = dot(x_hi, w_hi, acc) ``` All of x_lo, x_hi, w_lo, w_hi are live before the dots. That's 4 tiles + acc. For BM=32, BK_HALF=64, BN=128: x_lo (32,64), x_hi (32,64), w_lo (64,128), w_hi (64,128), acc (32,128). That's a lot live. If I fuse: compute w_lo, dot, then w_hi, dot — w_lo can be freed before w_hi. But Triton might not. Alternatively, restructure to interleave. Hard to control in Triton. Let me try a different structure: process lo and hi in SEPARATE loop passes? No, that doubles loads. Hmm. Let me reconsider — maybe just use a smaller tile for M=32 that fits better. Let me design the CUDA wmma W4A16 GEMM: - Output tile per block: (BM, BN). BM ∈ {16, 32}, BN = multiple of 16. - Use wmma 16x16x16 bf16 fragments. - Warps tile the output: e.g., 4 warps, each owns a (16, BN/4) or (BM/2, BN/2) sub-tile. - K-loop: for each group (128 K = 8 wmma-K-steps of 16), load x[BM, 128] and w_packed[64, BN], dequant w to bf16, mma 8 times (K steps of 16). This is involved. The dequant: w_packed (64, BN) → w_bf (128, BN) [unpack even/odd], then per-group subtract/multiply. Then mma. The wmma expects fragments loaded from memory. So I'd dequant into shared memory (w_bf in shared), then wmma::load_matrix from shared. Layout in shared: w_bf[128, BN] bf16 = 128*BN*2 bytes. For BN=128, that's 32KB shared. Plus x[BM, 128] = BM*256 bytes. Feasible (shared mem ~228KB on Blackwell, but per-block limit ~100KB). Hmm, this is getting complex but doable. Let me reconsider the warp tiling and mma. Actually, this is a LOT of careful CUDA. Given the wedge risk and time, let me reconsider once more whether it's worth it vs finalizing at 0.32. Design: - Block handles N-tile BN, all M rows. - Load x[0:M, :] is too big (M*K). Instead, stream K. - For each K-group: load x[0:M, k:k+128] into shared (M*128 bf16 = M*256 bytes, small). Load w_packed[64, BN], dequant to w_bf[128, BN] in shared/registers. Compute y[0:M, n] += x @ w for n in tile. - The compute y[M, BN] += x[M,128] @ w[128,BN]: this is a (M,128)x(128,BN) matmul. Let me design carefully: Tile: BM=32 (covers M=32, or 2 tiles for M=... no, M=32 → BM=32 one tile; M=16 → BM=16). BN=128. wmma: m16n16k16. For BM=32, BN=128: 2×8 = 16 wmma output tiles. Assign to warps: with 4 warps, each warp owns 4 wmma tiles (e.g., a 32×32 or 16×64 region). Hmm, 4 warps × 4 tiles = 16 tiles. Standard wmma tiling: each warp computes a (WM, WN) sub-tile using wmma. E.g., warp computes (16, 64) = 1×4 wmma tiles. 4 warps cover (32, 64) or (64, 32)... Let me think. For BM=32, BN=128 with 4 warps: each warp does (16, 64)? No, 4 warps × (16,64) = covers (32,128) if arranged 2×2: warps 0,1 top row (16,64) each, warps 2,3 bottom. So each warp owns (16,64) = 1 m-tile × 4 n-tiles (16×16). 4 wmma acc fragments per warp. K-loop: for k-step of 16 (8 steps per group of 128): - load x[BM, k:k+16] → shared (BM*16 bf16 = BM*32 bytes). - load w_packed dequant → w_bf[k:k+16, BN] → shared (16*BN bf16 = 16*BN*2 bytes). - each warp loads its x and w fragments, mma. The dequant: w_bf for k-step needs the unpacked+dequanted values. The group is 128 K = 8 k-steps. Scale/zero per group, constant within the 8 steps. So dequant w_bf = (unpack - z)*s with z, s from the group. This is implementable but detailed. The fragment loads (wmma::load_matrix) need row/col major layout in shared mem. Let me reconsider — for M=16, 32, what if each warp computes 1 output (m, n) and I structure it so adjacent warps (different m, same n) share the weight read via L1/L2 cache? The weight w[:,n] (column n) would be read by M warps (one per m). If those M warps run close in time and the column is L2-cached... but columns are strided (bad for coalescing in the original layout). With wqt (transposed, N×K//2), column n of original = row n of wqt (contiguous!). So wqt[n, :] is contiguous. M warps for the same n (different m) all read wqt[n, :] — same data! L1/L2 caches it. So the FIRST warp reads it from DRAM, the rest from cache. Effective weight read ≈ once (from DRAM) + M-1 cache hits. So: each warp computes 1 output (m, n), reading wqt[n, :] (contiguous) and x[m, :]. For M=32, N=12288 → 32*12288 = 393K warps. That's a lot but OK. The weight reuse via cache: for a given n, 32 warps (m=0..31) read wqt[n,:]. If co-scheduled, 31 cache hits. But the cache reuse depends on scheduling — warps for the same n must run concurrently. If I assign consecutive warps to consecutive m (same n), a block could handle all M for a few n's. E.g., block handles n-tile of BN n-columns, all M rows: M*BN warps. For M=32, BN=4 → 128 warps/block (too many). BN=1 → 32 warps/block. Hmm. Actually, this is the GEMM tiling again. The cache-reuse-via-adjacent-warps idea is essentially what shared-memory tiling does explicitly (and more reliably). OK, I'll commit to writing the wmma GEMM. Let me write it carefully. For k-step in 0..7 (16 K each): wmma::load x frag [BM_warp, 16], w frag [16, BN_warp], mma. The dequant writes 128*BN bf16 to shared per group. For BN=128, 128*128*2 = 32KB. Plus x BM*128*2. Wait — but that's "dequant-then-matmul" which the PROMPT says wastes bandwidth. For M=32, the dequant produces a 25MB bf16 weight matrix (4× the int4), then matmul reads it. That's 100MB of weight traffic vs 25MB int4. Way worse bandwidth. So no. Unless the dequanted weight stays in L2/registers... 25MB bf16 > L2 (96MB? no, 96MB L2). Actually 25MB fits in L2! So dequant to a 25MB bf16 buffer (in L2), then matmul reads from L2. The DRAM traffic = read int4 weight once (25MB) + write bf16 to L2 (but L2 writeback...). Hmm, the bf16 buffer is written to DRAM (unless it fits and stays in L2). 25MB fits in L2 (96MB). If it stays in L2, the matmul reads from L2 (fast). But the write of 25MB bf16 goes to L2 then maybe DRAM. Actually, cuBLAS matmul (torch.matmul) for bf16 is highly optimized (tensor cores, high occupancy). If I dequant to bf16 (25MB) then torch.matmul, the matmul would be fast. The dequant is a separate kernel (read int4, write bf16). Total DRAM: dequant reads 25MB int4 + writes 25MB bf16 = 50MB; matmul reads bf16 from L2 (if cached) + reads x + writes y. But the PROMPT explicitly forbids this pattern conceptually ("a separate dequant-then-matmul wastes the entire bandwidth advantage of int4"). And it forbids `torch.nn.functional.linear` (but not torch.matmul). Hmm, torch.matmul isn't forbidden. But the spirit is fused. Also, the dequant-then-matmul would be slower (2× weight traffic). Let me write it. Let me be efficient and write a correct wmma W4A16 GEMM. Let me write the kernel carefully. I'll target BM=32 (M=32) and BM=16 (M=16), BN=128, with wmma 16x16x16. Let me reconsider — actually, the cleanest small-M CUDA GEMM might be: each block handles a (M, BN) tile where the M rows are ALL handled by the block (M small). The weight w[:, BN_tile] is loaded once to shared, reused across M rows. The compute is M × (K × BN) FMAs. For M=32, BN=64: per K-step, 32×64 = 2048 FMAs. Without tensor cores, FMA is too slow (computed earlier). So I need tensor cores for the (M, K) × (K, BN) matmul. So tensor cores are unavoidable. wmma it is. OK let me just write the wmma GEMM. Final answer. Let me go. Let me write it carefully. I'll use wmma with a clean tiling. Let me write for BM=32, BN=128 (covering shape1 M=32 well), and BM=16 for shape4. Let me write the kernel. Key parts: 1. Shared mem: x_s[BM, 128] (per group), w_s[128, BN] (dequanted, per group). 2. Dequant: threads collaboratively unpack+dequant w_packed → w_s. 3. wmma K-loop: 8 steps of K=16, each: load x frag (16×16), w frag (16×16), mma. Let me write it. The warp tiling: 8 warps. Output (32, 128). Each warp owns (16, 32) = 2 wmma tiles arranged in N. Warps arranged 2 (M) × 4 (N). warp_id = warp % 4 (N-col), warp_row = warp / 4 (0 or 1). Each warp owns m-offset warp_row*16, n-offset (warp%4)*32. For the K-loop (8 steps of 16 within a group of 128): - x frag: wmma::load_matrix(A, &x_s[warp_row*16 + ?, k_step*16], stride=128). Wait, x_s is (BM, 128) row-major (M rows, K=128 cols). For wmma A fragment (16×16) at (m_off, k_off): load from x_s[m_off:m_off+16, k_off:k_off+16], row-major, stride 128. - w frag: w_s is (128, BN) row-major (K=128 rows, BN cols). For wmma B fragment (16×16) at (k_off, n_off): load from w_s[k_off:k_off+16, n_off:n_off+16], row-major, stride BN. - C frag: acc (16×16) at (m_off, n_off). - mma: C += A × B. After all groups, store C frags to global y. The dequant of w_s: w_packed (64, BN) [K/2 rows, BN cols] → w_s (128, BN). For byte (kb, n) [kb=0..63], even K=2kb → w_s[2kb, n] = (lo - z)*s, odd K=2kb+1 → w_s[2kb+1, n] = (hi - z)*s. Group g = kb // 64... wait group = K//128 = (2kb)//128 = kb//64. For kb in 0..63 (one group of 64 bytes = 128 K), g = constant (the group). So within a group load, all 64 bytes share one g. ✓. scale/zero = sc[g, n], zz[g, n]. So dequant: for the group, load sc[g, :], zz[g, :] (BN values). Each thread dequants a chunk of w_packed → w_s. Threads: 256 (8 warps). w_packed is 64×BN bytes. For BN=128, 64*128 = 8192 bytes. 256 threads → 32 bytes/thread. Each thread dequants 32 bytes → 64 w_s elements (32 even + 32 odd). Manageable. This is implementable. Let me write it. It's ~120 lines. Let me be careful. Actually, this is a significant chunk of careful CUDA. Let me write it and test. Let me allocate the time. For M=16: 4 warps, 4 tiles (16x16 each) = (16, 64). For M=32: 8 warps, 8 tiles = (32, 64). The grid: (cdiv(M, BM)=1, cdiv(N, BN)). For N=12288, BN=64 → 192 blocks. For N=14336, BN=64 → 224 blocks. Good occupancy. Let me write this. Let me be careful with the wmma fragment types and shared mem. OK let me write it. I'll write the kernel with template on BM (16 or 32), BN=64, WARPS accordingly. Let me write the CUDA: ```cpp #include <cuda_bf16.h> #include <mma.h> #include <cstdint> using namespace nvcuda; template<int BM, int BN, int WARPS> __global__ void w4a16_gemm_kernel( const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ x, // (M, K) const uint8_t* __restrict__ wq, // (K/2, N) const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ sc, // (K/128, N) const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ zz, // (K/128, N) __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ y, // (M, N) int M, int N, int K) { constexpr int WMMA_M=16, WMMA_N=16, WMMA_K=16; constexpr int WN_TILES = BN / WMMA_N; // 4 for BN=64 constexpr int WM_TILES = BM / WMMA_M; // 1 or 2 // warp owns: wm = warp / WN_TILES (0..WM_TILES-1), wn = warp % WN_TILES int warp = threadIdx.x / 32; int lane = threadIdx.x % 32; int wm = warp / WN_TILES; int wn = warp % WN_TILES; int bm_idx = blockIdx.y; // M-tile int bn_idx = blockIdx.x; // N-tile int m0 = bm_idx * BM; int n0 = bn_idx * BN; // shared mem extern __shared__ __nv_bfloat16 smem[]; __nv_bfloat16* xs = smem; // (BM, 128) __nv_bfloat16* ws = smem + BM*128; // (128, BN) -- dequanted weights for the group // accumulator wmma::fragment<wmma::accumulator, WMMA_M, WMMA_N, WMMA_K, float> acc[WM_TILES > 1 ? WM_TILES : 1][... ]; // hmm, each warp owns WN_TILES fragments? No — let me reconsider. ``` Hmm wait, I mixed up the tiling. Let me redo. Each warp owns ONE wmma output tile (16x16). The block has WARPS = WM_TILES * WN_TILES warps, each owning one (16,16) tile. warp's m-tile = warp / WN_TILES, n-tile = warp % WN_TILES. So warp computes output [m0 + wm*16 : +16, n0 + wn*16 : +16]. For BM=32 (WM_TILES=2), BN=64 (WN_TILES=4): WARPS=8. ✓. For BM=16 (WM_TILES=1), BN=64 (WN_TILES=4): WARPS=4. ✓. Each warp has ONE acc fragment (16x16). Good, simpler. K-loop over groups (K/128 groups): ``` for g in 0..K/128: k0 = g*128 // load x[m0:m0+BM, k0:k0+128] -> xs // load+dequant wq[k0/2 : k0/2+64, n0:n0+BN] -> ws (128 x BN) __syncthreads() // wmma 8 K-steps for ks in 0..7: // 128/16 = 8 wmma::load_matrix_sync(a_frag, &xs[wm*16][ks*16], 128) // 16x16 from xs wmma::load_matrix_sync(b_frag, &ws[ks*16][wn*16], BN) // 16x16 from ws wmma::mma_sync(acc, a, b, acc) __syncthreads() store acc -> y ``` The x load: x is (M, K) row-major. xs (BM, 128). Threads cooperatively load BM*128 bf16. For BM=32, 4096 elements, 256 threads → 16/thread. The w dequant: wq (K/2, N). For the group, wq rows [k0/2 : k0/2+64] (64 rows), cols [n0:n0+BN]. Load 64*BN bytes. Dequant to ws (128, BN): ws[2*kb + e, n] for e in {0,1}. The dequant collaborative: each thread handles some (kb, n). Load sc[g, n0:n0+BN], zz[g, ...]. For byte (kb, n): ws[2*kb, n] = (lo - zz)*sc, ws[2*kb+1, n] = (hi - zz)*sc. This is implementable. Let me write it carefully. The bounds: M might not be a multiple of BM (but our M are 16, 32 = multiples of BM if BM=M). N=12288/14336 multiples of 64. K=4096 multiple of 128. So no bounds checking needed for our shapes (but I'll add for safety). Let me write the full kernel. Let me write it for BM=32 (shape1) and BM=16 (shape4). Actually, let me make BM a template param and instantiate for 16 and 32. BN=64 fixed. Let me write test_wmma.py and test correctness + speed. Let me go. Let me write it carefully.

command (4767 chars — TRUNCATED)
cat > test_wmma.py <<'EOF'
import torch
from torch.utils.cpp_extension import load_inline
src = r'''
#include <cuda_bf16.h>
#include <mma.h>
#include <cstdint>
using namespace nvcuda;

// W4A16 GEMM via wmma tensor cores. Block tile (BM, BN=64), one warp per
// 16x16 output fragment. Per group: dequant weight into shared, then 8 wmma
// K-steps (16 each). fp32 accumulator.
template<int BM>
__global__ void w4a16_gemm_kernel(
    const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ x, const uint8_t* __restrict__ wq,
    const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ sc, const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ zz,
    __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ y, int M, int N, int K) {
    constexpr int BN = 64, GROUP = 128, WK = 16;
    constexpr int WN_T = BN / 16;            // 4
    constexpr int WM_T = (BM == 32) ? 2 : 1; // 1 or 2
    constexpr int WARPS = WM_T * WN_T;
    int warp = threadIdx.x >> 5;
    int lane = threadIdx.x & 31;
    int wm = warp / WN_T;
    int wn = warp % WN_T;
    int m0 = blockIdx.y * BM;
    int n0 = blockIdx.x * BN;

    __shared__ __nv_bfloat16 xs[BM][GROUP];          // (BM, 128)
    __shared__ __nv_bfloat16 ws[GROUP][BN];          // (128, BN) dequanted

    wmma::fragment<wmma::accumulator, 16, 16, 16, float> acc;
    wmma::fill_fragment(acc, 0.f);
    wmma::fragment<wmma::matrix_a, 16, 16, 16, __nv_bfloat16, wmma::row_major> a;
    wmma::fragment<wmma::matrix_b, 16, 16, 16, __nv_bfloat16, wmma::row_major> b;

    int ng = K / GROUP;
    for (int g = 0; g < ng; g++) {
        int k0 = g * GROUP;
        // load x[m0:m0+BM, k0:k0+128] -> xs
        for (int i = lane; i < BM * GROUP; i += WARPS * 32) {
            int r = i / GROUP, c = i % GROUP;
            xs[r][c] = x[(m0 + r) * K + (k0 + c)];
        }
        // load+dequant wq[k0/2 : +64, n0:n0+BN] -> ws[0:128][0:BN]
        // scale/zero for this group, this N-tile
        __nv_bfloat16 sbuf[BN], zbuf[BN];
        // load scale/zero collaboratively into ws reuse? load per-thread below.
        for (int i = lane; i < 64 * BN; i += WARPS * 32) {
            int kb = i / BN;          // 0..63
            int n = i % BN;           // 0..BN-1
            float s = __bfloat162float(sc[g * N + n0 + n]);
            float z = __bfloat162float(zz[g * N + n0 + n]);
            unsigned int bytev = wq[(k0 / 2 + kb) * N + n0 + n];
            float lo = (float)(bytev & 0xFu);
            float hi = (float)((bytev >> 4) & 0xFu);
            ws[2 * kb][n]     = __float2bfloat16((lo - z) * s);
            ws[2 * kb + 1][n] = __float2bfloat16((hi - z) * s);
        }
        __syncthreads();
        #pragma unroll
        for (int ks = 0; ks < GROUP / WK; ks++) {   // 8 steps
            wmma::load_matrix_sync(a, &xs[wm * 16][ks * WK], GROUP);
            wmma::load_matrix_sync(b, &ws[ks * WK][wn * 16], BN);
            wmma::mma_sync(acc, a, b, acc);
        }
        __syncthreads();
    }
    wmma::store_matrix_sync(&y[(m0 + wm * 16) * N + n0 + wn * 16], acc, N, wmma::mem_row_major);
}

void launch(torch::Tensor x, torch::Tensor wq, torch::Tensor sc, torch::Tensor zz, torch::Tensor out, int bm) {
    int M = out.size(0), N = out.size(1), K = x.size(1);
    if (bm == 32) {
        w4a16_gemm_kernel<32><<<dim3(N/64, (M+31)/32), 256>>>(reinterpret_cast<const __nv_bfloat16*>(x.data_ptr()),
            reinterpret_cast<const uint8_t*>(wq.data_ptr()), reinterpret_cast<const __nv_bfloat16*>(sc.data_ptr()),
            reinterpret_cast<const __nv_bfloat16*>(zz.data_ptr()), reinterpret_cast<__nv_bfloat16*>(out.data_ptr()), M, N, K);
    } else {
        w4a16_gemm_kernel<16><<<dim3(N/64, (M+15)/16), 128>>>(reinterpret_cast<const __nv_bfloat16*>(x.data_ptr()),
            reinterpret_cast<const uint8_t*>(wq.data_ptr()), reinterpret_cast<const __nv_bfloat16*>(sc.data_ptr()),
            reinterpret_cast<const __nv_bfloat16*>(zz.data_ptr()), reinterpret_cast<__nv_bfloat16*>(out.data_ptr()), M, N, K);
    }
}
'''
mod = load_inline(name="w4wmma", cpp_sources="void launch(torch::Tensor x, torch::Tensor wq, torch::Te

The memory layout: store_matrix_sync(float* ref, frag, ld, layout). For a 16x16 frag at position (wm*16, wn*16) in a (BM, BN) float array with stride BN: `&csm[(wm*16)*BN + wn*16]`, ld=BN. Then convert csm (BM*BN floats) → y (bf16). csm size = BM*BN*4 bytes. For BM=32, BN=64: 32*64*4 = 8KB. OK in shared. But I already use shared for xs (BM*128*2) and ws (128*BN*2). For BM=32: xs=32*128*2=8KB, ws=128*64*2=16KB, csm=8KB. Total 32KB shared/block. Blackwell SM has 228KB shared, per-block up to ~100KB. So 32KB is fine, allows ~7 blocks/SM (228/32) but register-limited. Good. Let me add csm and the convert. Let me fix the kernel. Actually, let me reconsider — instead of float acc → shared → convert, I could use a bf16 accumulator fragment. wmma supports bf16 accumulator? The accumulator for bf16 inputs is typically fp32. But wmma::accumulator with bf16 storage... Actually, you can have a bf16 accumulator fragment but precision suffers. For our tolerance (0.10), bf16 accumulator might be OK, but risky (large_activation). Let me keep fp32 acc and convert. Let me fix with the shared float buffer + convert. Let me rewrite the store: ``` __shared__ float csm[BM][BN]; // after K-loop: wmma::store_matrix_sync(&csm[wm*16][wn*16], acc, BN, wmma::mem_row_major); __syncthreads(); // convert csm -> y, collaborative for (int i = lane; i < BM*BN; i += WARPS*32) { int r = i / BN, c = i % BN; y[(m0+r)*N + n0+c] = __float2bfloat16(csm[r][c]); } ``` Let me update and test.

No — #undef should remove it regardless of -D. Unless the header checks the macro before my undef... Actually, the issue is `nvcuda::wmma::fragment` for bf16 — "incomplete type". This happens when the bf16 wmma specialization isn't available. On CUDA, bf16 wmma fragments are declared in `<mma.h>` but gated on `__CUDA_ARCH__ >= 800` AND the fragment specialization. Wait — the fragment variable is declared at function scope in a __global__ function. During device compilation (sm_120, __CUDA_ARCH__=1200), the bf16 specialization should be available. But "incomplete type" means it's not instantiated. Hmm. Let me reconsider — maybe CUDA 13's mma.h requires a different include or the bf16 wmma is in a separate header. Or maybe I need to NOT use row_major but the default layout. Actually — I recall that for some CUDA versions, `wmma::fragment<matrix_a, M, N, K, bfloat16, row_major>` requires `#include <cuda_bf16.h>` BEFORE `<mma.h>` AND the arch. I have that. Let me reconsider — maybe the issue is that the fragment type with bfloat16 and these exact dims (16,16,16) needs `compute_capability >= 80` which is met, but the COMPILATION for MULTIPLE archs (sm_75 is in the list!) causes issues. sm_75 (Turing) doesn't support bf16 wmma. The compilation includes `-gencode=arch=compute_75,code=sm_75`. When compiling for sm_75, the bf16 fragment is incomplete (not supported). That's the error! So the multi-arch compilation includes sm_75, where bf16 wmma isn't available → incomplete type. I need to compile ONLY for sm_120 (or >= sm_80). Actually, passing `-arch=sm_120` via extra_cuda_cflags should override. Let me try. Actually, the cleaner fix: guard the bf16 wmma code with `#if __CUDA_ARCH__ >= 800`. But the fragment declaration is at function scope; if I guard it, the kernel won't compile for sm_75 but will for sm_120. Since torch compiles for ALL listed archs, the sm_75 compilation fails. So I need to force single-arch compilation. Let me pass `extra_cuda_cflags=['-arch=sm_120a']` (or sm_120). Let me check what arch string. The device is sm_120 (compute capability 12.0). nvcc uses `-arch=sm_120`. Let me try `-arch=sm_120`. Actually, the gencode list has `compute_120,code=sm_120`. So sm_120 is valid. Actually, the simplest: guard with `#if __CUDA_ARCH__ >= 800` won't work for the fragment decl in a multi-arch build. args
{
  "command": "<544 chars \u2014 see below>",
  "description": "Force sm_120 arch for wmma",
  "timeout": 240000
}
command (544 chars)
uv run python - <<'PY'
s=open('test_wmma.py').read()
# add env set + extra flags before load_inline call
s=s.replace("mod = load_inline(name=\"w4wmma\",",
            "import os\nos.environ.setdefault('TORCH_CUDA_ARCH_LIST','12.0')\nmod = load_inline(\n    extra_cuda_cflags=['-arch=sm_120','--use_fast_math'],\n    name=\"w4wmma\",")
s=s.replace('cpp_sources="void launch','cpp_sources="void launch')  # noop
open('test_wmma.py','w').write(s)
print("patched")
PY
timeout 200 uv run python test_wmma.py 2>&1 | grep -E "M=|error|Error" | tail -8

args
{
  "command": "<1079 chars \u2014 see below>",

command (1079 chars)
cat > debug_wmma.py <<'EOF'
import torch
from torch.utils.cpp_extension import load_inline
exec(open('test_wmma.py').read().split('import reference')[0])  # compile mod
import reference
device='cuda:0'
M,N,K=32,64,128   # 1 tile, 1 group
reference.M,reference.N,reference.K=M,N,K
rm=reference.Model(M,N,K).to(device).eval()
wq=rm.w_q; sc=rm.scales; zr=rm.zeros
x=torch.randn(M,K,dtype=torch.bfloat16,device=device)
out=torch.empty(M,N,dtype=torch.bfloat16,device=device)
mod.launch(x,wq,sc,zr,out,32)
ref=rm(x)
print("out[0,:6]",out[0,:6].tolist())
print("ref[0,:6]",ref[0,:6].tolist())
print("out[4,:6]",out[4,:6].tolist())
print("ref[4,:6]",ref[4,:6].tolist())
# also check: is out == x @ ref_w? compute ref_w dequant
import torch
ng=K//128
wu=torch.empty(M if False else (K,N),dtype=torch.uint8,device=device)
wu[0::2]=wq&0xF; wu[1::2]=(wq>>4)&0xF
sf=sc.repeat_interleave(128,0); zf=zr.repeat_interleave(128,0)
wbf=(wu.to(torch.bfloat16)-zf)*sf
ref2=x@wbf
print("ref2[0,:6]",ref2[0,:6].tolist())
EOF
timeout 120 uv run python debug_wmma.py 2>&1 | grep -E "out\[|ref" | tail -8

232	        tl.store(out_ptr + offs_n, acc.to(tl.bfloat16), mask=mn)

236	
237	@triton.jit
238	def _reduce_cast_kernel(p_ptr, y_ptr, N, NSPLIT, stride_pk, BN: tl.constexpr):
239	    pid = tl.program_id(0)
240	    offs = pid * BN + tl.arange(0, BN)
241	    mask = offs < N
242	    acc = tl.zeros((BN,), dtype=tl.float32)
243	    for i in range(0, NSPLIT):
244	        acc += tl.load(p_ptr + i * stride_pk + offs, mask=mask, other=0.0)
245	    tl.store(y_ptr + offs, acc.to(tl.bfloat16), mask=mask)
246	
247	
248	def _gemv_plan(N, K):
249	    KH = K // 2
250	    BN = 32
251	    n_groups = KH // 64
252	    nnt = triton.cdiv(N, BN)
253	    if N >= 8192:
254	        GPT, BK, nw, ns = 2, 128, 4, 3
255	    else:
256	        GPT, BK, nw, ns = 1, 64, 4, 4
257	    # split-K to ~6x SMs total blocks (caps occupancy-driven stalls).
258	    nspl = max(1, (6 * _NUM_SMS) // nnt)
259	    nspl = min(nspl, n_groups)
260	    while nspl > 1 and (KH // nspl) % BK != 0:
261	        nspl -= 1
262	    SKB = KH // nspl
263	    return dict(BN=BN, GPT=GPT, BK=BK, nw=nw, ns=ns, nspl=nspl, SKB=SKB, nnt=nnt)
264	
265	
266	def _gemv(x, wqt, scales, zeros, N, K, plan, partial):
267	    KH = K // 2
268	    y = torch.empty((1, N), dtype=torch.bfloat16, device=x.device)
269	    _w4a16_gemv_kernel[(plan["nnt"], plan["nspl"])](
270	        x, wqt, scales, zeros, partial,
271	        N, KH,
272	        scales.stride(1), scales.st

285	# Prefill path: M > 1, tl.dot GEMM with fused dequant (original layout).
286	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
287	@triton.jit
288	def _w4a16_gemm_kernel(
289	    x_ptr, wq_ptr, s_ptr, z_ptr, y_ptr,
290	    M, N, K,
291	    stride_xm, stride_xk,
292	    stride_wpk, stride_wn,
293	    stride_sg, stride_sn,
294	    stride_ym, stride_yn,
295	    BM: tl.constexpr, BN: tl.constexpr, BK: tl.constexpr,
296	    GROUP: tl.constexpr,
297	):
298	    pid_m = tl.program_id(0)
299	    pid_n = tl.program_id(1)
300	
301	    offs_m = pid_m * BM + tl.arange(0, BM)
302	    offs_n = pid_n * BN + tl.arange(0, BN)
303	    offs_k = tl.arange(0, BK)
304	
305	    mask_m = offs_m < M
306	    mask_n = offs_n < N
307	
308	    acc = tl.zeros((BM, BN), dtype=tl.float32)
309	    n_groups = K // GROUP
310	    BK_HALF: tl.constexpr = BK // 2
311	
312	    for g in range(0, n_groups):
313	        k0 = g * GROUP
314	        x_ptrs = x_ptr + offs_m[:, None] * stride_xm + (k0 + offs_k)[None, :] * stride_xk
315	        x = tl.load(x_ptrs, mask=mask_m[:, None], other=0.0)
316	        x_r = tl.reshape(x, (BM, BK_HALF, 2))
317	        x_lo, x_hi = tl.split(x_r)
318	
319	        pk_offs = (k0 // 2) + tl.arange(0, BK_HALF)
320	        w_ptrs = wq_ptr + pk_offs[:, None] * stride_wpk + offs_n[None, :] * stride_wn
321	        w_packed = tl.load(w_ptrs, mask=mask_n[None, :], other=0)
322	        w_lo = (w_packed & 0xF).to(tl.bfloat16)
323	        w_hi = ((w_packed >> 4) & 0xF

From NVIDIA docs (wmma): - `wmma::fragment<matrix_b, M, N, K, T, layout>`: layout is row_major or col_major, describing how the application stores B in memory. - `mma_sync(d, a, b, c)`: d = a*b + c, standard matrix multiply where a is M×K, b is K×N. So if B is (K,N) row-major in memory, use row_major. That's what I did. So it SHOULD be correct. So my bug is elsewhere. The scrambled output... Let me reconsider the dequant once more. OH WAIT. I think I see it. The dequant loop: ``` for (int i = lane; i < 64 * BN; i += WARPS * 32) { int kb = i / BN; // 0..63 int n = i % BN; // 0..63 ``` But `BN` is a constexpr = 64. And the loop bounds `64 * BN` = 64*64 = 4096. But `kb = i / BN` — for i in 0..4095, kb = i/64 in 0..63. ✓. n = i%64. ✓. Hmm that's right. Let me reconsider the x load: ``` for (int i = lane; i < BM * GROUP; i += WARPS * 32) { int r = i / GROUP, c = i % GROUP; xs[r][c] = x[(m0 + r) * K + (k0 + c)]; } ``` GROUP=128. I recall that in practice, many wmma GEMM implementations for C = A @ B use: - A: row_major - B: col_major AND store B transposed. OR they compute C = A @ B^T. The reason: the mma instruction natively does D = A * B^T. So to compute C = A @ B, you either: (a) Load B as-is (K,N row-major) with row_major → mma sees it as B, computes A*B (wmma handles the transpose internally). (b) Pre-transpose B to (N,K) and load col_major. wmma docs claim (a) works with row_major. But there have been bugs/confusion. Let me just empirically test col_major. Let me recreate the test. command (4572 chars — TRUNCATED)
cat > test_wmma2.py <<'EOF'
import torch, os
os.environ.setdefault('TORCH_CUDA_ARCH_LIST','12.0')
from torch.utils.cpp_extension import load_inline
src = r'''
#undef __CUDA_NO_BFLOAT16_CONVERSIONS__
#include <cuda_bf16.h>
#include <mma.h>
#include <cstdint>
using namespace nvcuda;
template<int BM, int BLAYOUT>  // BLAYOUT 0=row,1=col for B
__global__ void gemm(const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ x, const uint8_t* __restrict__ wq,
    const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ sc, const __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ zz,
    __nv_bfloat16* __restrict__ y, int M, int N, int K) {
    constexpr int BN=64, GROUP=128, WK=16, WN_T=4;
    constexpr int WM_T = (BM==32)?2:1;
    constexpr int WARPS=WM_T*WN_T;
    int warp=threadIdx.x>>5, lane=threadIdx.x&31;
    int wm=warp/WN_T, wn=warp%WN_T;
    int m0=blockIdx.y*BM, n0=blockIdx.x*BN;
    __shared__ __nv_bfloat16 xs[BM][GROUP];
    __shared__ __nv_bfloat16 ws[GROUP][BN];
    __shared__ float csm[BM][BN];
    wmma::fragment<wmma::accumulator,16,16,16,float> acc;
    wmma::fill_fragment(acc,0.f);
    wmma::fragment<wmma::matrix_a,16,16,16,__nv_bfloat16,wmma::row_major> a;
    int ng=K/GROUP;
    for(int g=0;g<ng;g++){
        int k0=g*GROUP;
        for(int i=lane;i<BM*GROUP;i+=WARPS*32){int r=i/GROUP,c=i%GROUP; xs[r][c]=x[(m0+r)*K+(k0+c)];}
        for(int i=lane;i<64*BN;i+=WARPS*32){
            int kb=i/BN,n=i%BN;
            float s=__bfloat162float(sc[g*N+n0+n]); float z=__bfloat162float(zz[g*N+n0+n]);
            unsigned int bv=wq[(k0/2+kb)*N+n0+n];
            ws[2*kb][n]=__float2bfloat16(((float)(bv&0xFu)-z)*s);
            ws[2*kb+1][n]=__float2bfloat16(((float)((bv>>4)&0xFu)-z)*s);
        }
        __syncthreads();
        #pragma unroll
        for(int ks=0;ks<GROUP/WK;ks++){
            wmma::load_matrix_sync(a,&xs[wm*16][ks*WK],GROUP);
            if(BLAYOUT==0){
                wmma::fragment<wmma::matrix_b,16,16,16,__nv_bfloat16,wmma::row_major> b;
                wmma::load_matrix_sync(b,&ws[ks*WK][wn*16],BN);
                wmma::mma_sync(acc,a,b,acc);
            } else {
                wmma::fragment<wmma::matrix_b,16,16,16,__nv_bfloat16,wmma::col_major> b;
                wmma::load_matrix_sync(b,&ws[ks*WK][wn*16],GROUP);
                wmma::mma_sync(acc,a,b,acc);
            }
        }
        __syncthreads();
    }
    wmma::store_matrix_sync(&csm[wm*16][wn*16],acc,BN,wmma::mem_row_major);
    __syncthreads();
    for(int i=lane;i<BM*BN;i+=WARPS*32){int r=i/BN,c=i%BN; y[(m0+r)*N+n0+c]=__float2bfloat16(csm[r][c]);}
}
void launch(torch::Tensor x,torch::Tensor wq,torch::Tensor sc,torch::Tensor zz,torch::Tensor out,int bm,int bl){
    int M=out.size(0),N=out.size(1),K=x.size(1);
    if(bm==32){ if(bl==0) gemm<32,0><<<dim3(N/64,M/32),256>>>(r(x),r(wq),r(sc),r(zz),r(out),M,N,K); else gemm<32,1><<<dim3(N/64,M/32),256>>>(r(x),r(wq),r(sc),r(zz),r(out),M,N,K);}
    else { if(bl==0) gemm<16,0><<<dim3(N/64,M/16),128>>>(r(x),r(wq),r(sc),r(zz),r(out),M,N,K); else gemm<16,1><<<dim3(N/64,M/16),128>>>(r(x),r(wq),r(sc),r(zz),r(out),M,N,K);}
}
#define r(p) reinterpret_cast<const __nv_bfloat16*>(p.data_ptr())
'''
# fix: r() macro must be before launch. redo properly:
src=src.replace('#define r(p) reinterpret_cast<const __nv_bfloat16*>(p.data_ptr())\n','')
src=src.replace('void launch(torch::Tensor x,torch::Tensor wq,torch::Tensor sc,torch::Tensor zz,torch::Tensor out,int bm,int bl){',
'#define R(p) reinterpret_cast<const __nv_bfloat16*>(p.data_ptr())\nvoid launch(torch::Tensor x,torch::Tensor wq,torch::Tensor sc,torch::Tensor zz,torch::Tensor out,int bm,int bl){')
src=src.replace('r(out),M,N,K)','R(out),M,N,K)').replace('r(x),r(wq),r(sc),r(zz),','R(x),R(wq),R(sc),R(zz),')
mod=load_inline(name="w4wmma2",cpp_sources="void launch(torch::Tensor,torch::Tensor,torch::Tensor,torch::Tensor,torch::Tensor,int,int);",
    cuda_sources=src,functions=["launch"],extra_cuda_cflags=['-arch=sm_120'],verbose=False)
import reference
device='cuda:0'
for M,N,K in [(32,12288,4096),(16,14336,4096)]:
    reference.

assistant
Write /home/agent/.claude/projects/-workspace-problems-07-w4a16-gemm/memory/cuda-load-inline-wedge.md
diff (15 lines)
--- /dev/null
+++ b//home/agent/.claude/projects/-workspace-problems-07-w4a16-gemm/memory/cuda-load-inline-wedge.md
@@ -0,0 +1,12 @@
+---
+name: cuda-load-inline-wedge
+description: Concurrent torch load_inline CUDA compiles wedge the bash shell in this env
+metadata:
+  type: feedback
+---
+
+Running multiple `torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline` builds concurrently (e.g. several background `uv run python test_*.py` each compiling a distinct named extension) wedges the shell: every new command auto-backgrounds and never returns, `nvidia-smi`/`echo` time out, the system stays unresponsive for 10+ minutes until the compiles drain or processes are killed.
+
+**Why:** Each `load_inline` spawns nvcc (CPU/RAM-heavy, ~20-40s); several at once saturate the box. A buggy CUDA kernel that hangs at `cudaDeviceSynchronize` (infinite loop) will also wedge the GPU and look identical.
+
+**How to apply:** Compile ONE extension at a time — wait for each `uv run` to finish before launching the next. Avoid fire-and-forget background compile sweeps. If wedged: `pkill -9 -f "uv run"` / the python PID (the kill command itself may need a patient retry). Tested env: RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell SM120, torch 2.11+cu130, triton 3.6.
args
{
  "file_path": "/home/agent/.claude/projects/-workspace-problems-07-w4a16-gemm/memory/cuda-load-inline-wedge.md",
  "content": "<1079 chars \u2014 see diff>"
}

Write /home/agent/.claude/projects/-workspace-problems-07-w4a16-gemm/memory/w4a16-kernel-findings.md
diff (23 lines)
--- /dev/null
+++ b//home/agent/.claude/projects/-workspace-problems-07-w4a16-gemm/memory/w4a16-kernel-findings.md
@@ -0,0 +1,20 @@
+---
+name: w4a16-kernel-findings
+description: W4A16 GEMM perf findings on SM120 Blackwell (07_w4a16_gemm problem)
+metadata:
+  type: reference
+---
+
+Tuned W4A16 (AWQ-style asymmetric int4, group=128) GEMM for RTX PRO 6000 (SM120, 1.8 TB/s, 200 bf16 TFLOPS, 188 SM, 96MB L2). Scored by DRAM-bandwidth fraction (regime=memory). Geomean ~0.32 achieved (ceiling 0.68; M=256 is compute-capped at 0.15).
+
+What worked:
+- **Decode (M=1): CUDA GEMV on TRANSPOSED weight (N, K//2)**, one warp per output, each lane reads uint4=16B=32 int4, warp shuffle-reduce; prefetch next iter (1-deep for large N, 2-deep for small N to add memory-level parallelism). Single kernel, ~1000 GB/s (M=1 N=12288) vs Triton tl.sum GEMV ~750. Reorder weight once at load (w_q.t().contiguous()), cache buffers, return persistent output to cut per-call overhead.
+- **Prefill (M≥16): Triton tl.dot GEMM**, fused dequant, even/odd-K nibble split so each packed byte is read once. `num_warps=8` roughly DOUBLES M=16/M=32 bandwidth. M=256 hits 168 TFLOPS (BM=64 BN=128 nw=8 ns=3).
+
+What didn't / gotchas:
+- Triton GEMM for small M is register-bound (166 regs/thread → 16% occupancy → ~640 GB/s); BK=64 to cut regs made it worse (lower arithmetic intensity).
+- bf16 `nvcuda::wmma` via load_inline fails: torch passes `-D__CUDA_NO_BFLOAT16_CONVERSIONS__` and a multi-arch gencode list incl. sm_75 → "incomplete type" for bf16 fragments. Force `os.environ['TORCH_CUDA_ARCH_LIST']='12.0'` + `extra_cuda_cflags=['-arch=sm_120']`. Even then a from-scratch wmma W4A16 GEMM gave scrambled output (couldn't quickly debug) — CUTLASS mixed-input or more wmma care would be the path to lift M=16/32 past 0.45.
+- split-K + reduce kernel hurt decode (overhead > block-count benefit); a single direct-store kernel won.
+- For M=1, tl.dot needs M≥16 (padding wastes 16× compute and loses to the scalar GEMV).
+
+See [[cuda-load-inline-wedge]].
args

  "file_path": "/home/agent/.claude/projects/-workspace-problems-07-w4a16-gemm/memory/w4a16-kernel-findings.md",
  "content": "<1885 chars \u2014 see diff>"
}

The W4A16 GEMM is complete. Here's the final result. ## Summary `solution.py` is a real fused unpack+dequant+GEMM kernel (no banned ops), passes `python check.py` (all 5 shapes × 3 seeds × numeric-stress cases), and benchmarks at **geomean peak_fraction ≈ 0.32** (2.3× over the reference-style baseline of 0.14). **Per-shape (DRAM-bandwidth fraction, regime=memory):** | shape | M | N | K | path | frac | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 1 | 12288 | 4096 | CUDA GEMV | **0.55** (994 GB/s) | | 1 | 32 | 12288 | 4096 | Triton GEMM | 0.36 | | 2 | 256 | 12288 | 4096 | Triton GEMM | 0.13 (168 TFLOPS, near 0.15 compute ceiling) | | 3 | 1 | 4096 | 4096 | CUDA GEMV | 0.30 | | 4 | 16 | 14336 | 4096 | Triton GEMM | 0.45 | **Two kernel paths:** - **Decode (M=1): CUDA C++ GEMV via `load_inline`** on a one-time-**transposed** weight `(N, K//2)`, one warp per output, 32 lanes doing K/32 of the dot product + warp shuffle-reduce, vectorized 16-byte loads with next-iteration prefetch (1-deep for large N, 2-deep for small N to add memory-level parallelism). This broke the ~750 GB/s ceiling that capped a Triton `tl.sum`-based GEMV (ncu showed the Triton path was stall-bound at 32% occupancy). - **Prefill (M≥16): Triton `tl.dot` GEMM** with fused per-group dequant, even/odd-K nibble split so each packed byte is read once. `num_warps=8` roughly doubled M=16/32 bandwidth. Buffers (transposed weight, output, partials) are cached on the module and returned persistently to cut per-call launch overhead. **What I couldn't crack (the remaining gap):** the M=16/32 Triton GEMM is register-bound (166 regs/thread → 16% occupancy). I tried a hand-written bf16 `wmma` tensor-core GEMM but hit torch's `-D__CUDA_NO_BFLOAT16_CONVERSIONS__` + multi-arch `sm_75` "incomplete type" issue, then scrambled output I couldn't quickly debug — so I kept the Triton GEMM rather than risk the working solution. CUTLASS mixed-input GEMM or a more careful wmma implementation is the path to lift those two shapes further. One process-hygiene lesson worth flagging: I accidentally wedged the shell by running several `load_inline` CUDA compiles concurrently — saved to memory so that doesn't recur.
